description: After a person, home, hope, or way of life is lost, someone may 'nuhemi': grieve or mourn over time. 'nuhe' (sadness) may have no identified loss and may pass quickly, while 'nuhewa' (weep) names tears rather than the process around them. A person can grieve without weeping, and tears can come for reasons other than grief.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and raise the back of the tongue for high-back rounded 'u'. Let unvoiced air pass through the open glottis into stressed middle-height front unrounded 'e'. Close the lips for voiced 'm', keep the airflow nasal, and release into high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: 'nuhemi' contains the full sound of 'nuhe' (sadness) and continues for one more syllable, closing through nasal 'mi'. That audible kinship keeps grief easy to remember beside the shorter sadness form.
usage_notes: The person who grieves is the subject. The preposition 'wei' introduces a person or being grieved for; a separate cause clause can identify another kind of loss. Its event noun covers grief, mourning, or a period of grieving.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Grief brings attachment and impermanence into the same lived moment. 'nuhemi' gives that process time without treating it as a failure of attention or practice.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Mourning customs can give loss a season and companions. 'nuhemi' names the grieving while the particular community keeps the form of its observance.

semantic_domains.emotion: the continuing process of grief after a loss
semantic_domains.ritual: mourning carried through a funeral, memorial, or repeated observance
